project.name: PayFlow SaaS Platform
project.description: A multi-tenant SaaS platform for subscription billing and payment processing, built on Stripe.

specifications.title: Stripe Webhook Handler
specifications.description: Implement a robust Stripe webhook ingestion pipeline that receives, validates, and processes Stripe events. The handler must be idempotent, auditable, and support all critical payment lifecycle events.
specifications.content:
# Stripe Webhook Handler

This specification covers the end-to-end implementation of a Stripe webhook handler for the PayFlow platform. The system must securely receive webhook events from Stripe, verify their signatures, deduplicate them, and dispatch them to domain-specific processors.

## Overview

Stripe sends webhook events for payment intents, subscription changes, invoice updates, and dispute notifications. Our handler must process these reliably with at-least-once delivery semantics and idempotent processing.
specifications.background: PayFlow currently polls the Stripe API every 60 seconds for payment updates, causing delays of up to 2 minutes for subscription activations. Customers have reported confusion when their dashboard does not reflect successful payments immediately. Switching to webhook-driven processing will reduce latency to under 5 seconds.
specifications.scope: Webhook endpoint, signature verification, event deduplication, event routing to domain processors (payments, subscriptions, invoices, disputes). Does NOT include Stripe Connect or marketplace payouts.
goals: Process Stripe webhook events within 5 seconds of receipt
Support idempotent processing for all event types
Provide full audit trail for every webhook event
requirements: Verify Stripe-Signature header using webhook signing secret
Deduplicate events using Stripe event ID
Route events to type-specific handler functions
Store raw event payload for audit purposes
Return 200 status within 3 seconds to avoid Stripe retries
Handle at least 100 concurrent webhook deliveries
nonFunctionalRequirements: Zero data loss — all events must be persisted before acknowledgment
Graceful degradation if downstream processors are slow
Structured JSON logging for all webhook events
acceptanceCriteria: Stripe test webhooks are processed end-to-end in staging environment
Duplicate event IDs are rejected with 200 status and logged
Invalid signatures return 401 and are logged as security events
All 8 supported event types route to correct processors
Integration tests pass with Stripe CLI webhook forwarding
successMetrics: Zero duplicate side effects in a 30-day window
Customer-facing payment status updates within 5 seconds
guardrails: Never expose Stripe signing secrets in logs or error responses
Never modify payment amounts or currencies in webhook handlers
Always return 200 for duplicate events to prevent Stripe retries
Never block the HTTP response on downstream processing
constraints: Must use existing Express.js application server
Must use PostgreSQL for event storage (existing database)
Must be deployable without infrastructure changes
assumptions: Stripe webhook signing secret is available via environment variable STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET
PostgreSQL connection pool is already configured in the application
Existing authentication middleware does NOT apply to webhook endpoints
Stripe will retry failed deliveries up to 3 times over 72 hours
risks: High webhook volume during flash sales could overwhelm the database connection pool
Clock skew between Stripe and our servers could cause signature verification failures
Long-running event processors could cause webhook timeout and retries
specifications.architecture: The webhook handler follows a three-stage pipeline: (1) Ingestion — raw HTTP receipt, signature verification, and immediate 200 response; (2) Persistence — event stored in webhook_events table with status 'received'; (3) Processing — async dispatch to domain handlers via an in-process event queue. Each stage is independently recoverable.
specifications.fileStructure:
src/
  webhooks/
    stripe/
      handler.ts          # Express route handler
      signature.ts        # Signature verification
      deduplication.ts    # Event dedup logic
      router.ts           # Event type routing
      processors/
        payment.ts        # payment_intent.* handlers
        subscription.ts   # customer.subscription.* handlers
        invoice.ts        # invoice.* handlers
        dispute.ts        # charge.dispute.* handlers
      types.ts            # TypeScript types
      constants.ts        # Event type constants
  database/
    migrations/
      20240301_webhook_events.ts
    repositories/
      webhookEventRepository.ts

POST /api/webhooks/stripe.requestBody: Raw Stripe event JSON (must be parsed from raw body, not JSON middleware)

codeStandards.naming: camelCase for variables/functions, PascalCase for types/interfaces
codeStandards.errorHandling: Result pattern — never throw in business logic, only at boundaries
codeStandards.logging: Structured JSON via application logger, include correlationId on every log line

returnTypes.Result: type Result<T, E = Error> = { ok: true; value: T } | { ok: false; error: E }
returnTypes.AsyncResult: type AsyncResult<T, E = Error> = Promise<Result<T, E>>

epics.title: Webhook Ingestion Pipeline
epics.description: Build the HTTP endpoint, signature verification, event deduplication, and persistence layer for incoming Stripe webhooks.
epics.content:
# Epic 1: Webhook Ingestion Pipeline

This epic covers everything from the raw HTTP request arriving at our server to the event being safely stored in the database. The focus is on security (signature verification), reliability (deduplication), and speed (respond to Stripe quickly).
epics.objective: Establish a secure, reliable ingestion path that receives Stripe webhooks, verifies authenticity, deduplicates, persists, and acknowledges within 500ms.
epics.scope: HTTP endpoint, signature verification, deduplication, database persistence, raw body parsing
goals: Verify every webhook signature before processing
Persist every unique event to the database
acceptanceCriteria: POST /api/webhooks/stripe returns 200 for valid events
Invalid signatures return 401
Duplicate event IDs return 200 without re-processing
Events are stored in webhook_events table with raw payload
guardrails: Raw body must be preserved for signature verification — do not apply JSON parsing middleware
Never log full event payloads in production (may contain PII)
constraints: Must use raw body buffer for Stripe signature verification
Database write must complete before returning 200
assumptions: Express raw body middleware can be applied per-route
PostgreSQL can handle the write throughput
risks: Express JSON middleware may consume the raw body before signature verification
epics.architecture: Linear pipeline: Raw HTTP -> Signature Verify -> Dedup Check -> DB Persist -> 200 Response

tickets.title: Create webhook_events database table and repository
tickets.description: Create the database migration for the webhook_events table and implement the repository layer for CRUD operations on webhook events.
tickets.notes: The table must support high write throughput. Add an index on stripe_event_id for deduplication lookups. Consider partitioning by created_at if volume exceeds 1M events/month.
acceptanceCriteria: Migration creates webhook_events table with all required columns
Unique constraint on stripe_event_id prevents duplicates
Repository supports insert, findByStripeEventId, and updateStatus
Migration rolls back cleanly

steps: Create Knex migration file for webhook_events table
Define columns: id (uuid PK), stripe_event_id (unique), event_type, payload (jsonb), status, created_at, processed_at
Add indexes on stripe_event_id and event_type
Create WebhookEventRepository class with insert, findByStripeEventId, updateStatus methods
Write unit tests for repository methods
implementation.notes: Use Knex migration API. The jsonb column for payload allows efficient querying of event data.

codeReferences.name: Migration table schema
Repository interface

technicalDetails.partitioning: Not required for v1 — revisit at 1M events/month
technicalDetails.indexStrategy: B-tree on stripe_event_id for O(log n) dedup lookups

typeReferences.name: WebhookEvent

commands: npx knex migrate:latest --env test && npx knex migrate:rollback --env test
gates: All repository CRUD operations pass with test database
Migration applies and rolls back without errors
Duplicate stripe_event_id insert throws unique constraint error

tickets.title: Implement webhook endpoint with signature verification and deduplication
tickets.description: Create the Express route handler for POST /api/webhooks/stripe with Stripe signature verification, event deduplication using the repository, and immediate 200 acknowledgment.
tickets.notes: The raw body must be preserved for signature verification. Express JSON middleware will consume the body buffer, so use express.raw() middleware on this route only. Return 200 as fast as possible to avoid Stripe retries.
acceptanceCriteria: Valid Stripe webhooks return 200 with { received: true }
Invalid signatures return 401
Duplicate events return 200 without re-processing
Events are persisted to database before response
Raw body is used for signature verification (not parsed JSON)

steps: Create signature.ts with verifyStripeSignature function using Stripe SDK
Create deduplication.ts with isDuplicate function using WebhookEventRepository
Create handler.ts with Express route handler that orchestrates verify -> dedup -> persist -> respond
Register route in Express app with express.raw() middleware
Write integration tests using Supertest with Stripe test fixtures
implementation.notes: Use stripe.webhooks.constructEvent() for signature verification — it handles timestamp tolerance and signature comparison securely.

codeReferences.name: Signature verification function
Route handler skeleton

technicalDetails.signatureAlgorithm: HMAC-SHA256 with timestamp tolerance of 300 seconds
technicalDetails.bodyParsing: Must use express.raw({ type: 'application/json' }) — not express.json()
technicalDetails.responseStrategy: Return 200 before async processing to meet Stripe timeout requirements

typeReferences.name: StripeWebhookConfig

gates: Duplicate event ID returns 200 without database insert
Response time under 500ms for valid events

epics.title: Event Processing and Domain Routing
epics.description: Implement the event routing layer that dispatches verified webhook events to domain-specific processors for payments, subscriptions, invoices, and disputes.
epics.content:
# Epic 2: Event Processing and Domain Routing

Once events are ingested and persisted, they must be routed to the correct domain processor. Each processor handles a family of Stripe event types and translates them into domain actions (e.g., activating a subscription, recording a payment).
epics.objective: Build an extensible event routing system with domain processors for all critical Stripe event types, ensuring idempotent processing and proper error handling.
epics.scope: Event type router, payment processor, subscription processor, invoice processor, dispute processor
goals: Route all supported event types to correct processors
Handle processor failures without losing events
acceptanceCriteria: payment_intent.succeeded events update payment records
customer.subscription.updated events update subscription status
invoice.payment_failed events trigger dunning notifications
charge.dispute.created events create dispute records
Unknown event types are logged and marked as skipped
guardrails: Processors must be idempotent — re-processing an event must not create duplicate side effects
Processor failures must update event status to 'failed', not crash the application
constraints: Processors run in-process (no external queue in v1)
Must not exceed existing database connection pool limits
assumptions: Notification service is available for dunning emails
risks: Long-running processors could block the event queue
Domain service failures could cascade to webhook processing
epics.architecture: Router pattern: EventRouter maps event type prefixes to processor instances. Each processor implements the IEventProcessor interface with a process(event) method returning AsyncResult<void>.

tickets.title: Implement event type router
tickets.description: Create the EventRouter class that maps Stripe event type prefixes to processor instances and dispatches events for async processing.
tickets.notes: The router should use a Map<string, IEventProcessor> for O(1) lookup. Support prefix matching so 'payment_intent.*' routes to the PaymentProcessor.
acceptanceCriteria: Router dispatches payment_intent.* events to PaymentProcessor
Router dispatches customer.subscription.* events to SubscriptionProcessor
Router dispatches invoice.* events to InvoiceProcessor
Router dispatches charge.dispute.* events to DisputeProcessor
Unknown event types log a warning and return success

steps: Define IEventProcessor interface with process(event: StripeWebhookEvent): AsyncResult<void>
Create EventRouter class with register(prefix, processor) and dispatch(event) methods
Implement prefix matching logic for event type to processor lookup
Add structured logging for dispatch events
Write unit tests with mock processors
implementation.notes: Keep the router simple — no dependency injection framework needed. Constructor takes processor map.

codeReferences.name: IEventProcessor interface
EventRouter dispatch

technicalDetails.routingStrategy: Prefix match on event type string (e.g., 'payment_intent' matches 'payment_intent.succeeded')
technicalDetails.concurrency: Single-threaded dispatch in v1, async processing within each processor

gates: Correct processor called for each event type prefix
Unknown event types do not throw
Processor errors are captured in Result, not thrown

tickets.title: Implement payment and subscription event processors
tickets.description: Create the PaymentProcessor and SubscriptionProcessor classes that handle payment_intent.* and customer.subscription.* events respectively, translating Stripe events into domain actions.
tickets.notes: Both processors must be idempotent. Use the Stripe event ID combined with event type as the idempotency key. Check if the domain entity already reflects the event state before applying changes.
acceptanceCriteria: payment_intent.succeeded creates or updates payment record with status 'completed'
payment_intent.payment_failed updates payment record with status 'failed'
customer.subscription.created creates subscription record
customer.subscription.updated updates subscription status and period dates
customer.subscription.deleted marks subscription as canceled
Re-processing the same event produces no additional side effects

steps: Create PaymentProcessor implementing IEventProcessor
Implement handlePaymentIntentSucceeded and handlePaymentIntentFailed methods
Create SubscriptionProcessor implementing IEventProcessor
Implement handleSubscriptionCreated, handleSubscriptionUpdated, handleSubscriptionDeleted methods
Add Zod schemas for Stripe event payload validation
Add idempotency checks in each handler
Write unit tests with fixture data from Stripe
implementation.notes: Use Stripe's TypeScript types for event payloads. Validate with Zod at runtime as defense-in-depth.

codeReferences.name: PaymentProcessor skeleton
Idempotency check pattern

technicalDetails.idempotencyStrategy: Check domain entity state before applying changes. If already in target state, skip and return success.
technicalDetails.payloadValidation: Zod schemas validate Stripe event payload structure at runtime
technicalDetails.domainServices: PaymentService and SubscriptionService are injected via constructor

typeReferences.name: PaymentRecord
SubscriptionRecord

gates: All event subtypes route to correct handler method
Idempotent re-processing produces no duplicate records
Invalid payloads are caught by Zod validation and returned as errors
Domain service is called with correct parameters

blueprints.title: Stripe Webhook Architecture
blueprints.description: High-level architecture diagram showing the webhook ingestion pipeline from Stripe to domain processors.
blueprints.content:
graph TB
    Stripe[Stripe API] -->|POST /api/webhooks/stripe| LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> Express[Express Server]
    
    subgraph Ingestion Pipeline
        Express --> RawBody[Raw Body Parser]
        RawBody --> SigVerify[Signature Verification]
        SigVerify -->|Invalid| Reject[401 Reject]
        SigVerify -->|Valid| Dedup[Deduplication Check]
        Dedup -->|Duplicate| Ack200[200 Acknowledge]
        Dedup -->|New Event| Persist[Persist to DB]
        Persist --> Ack200
    end
    
    subgraph Event Processing
        Persist --> Router[Event Router]
        Router --> PaymentProc[Payment Processor]
        Router --> SubProc[Subscription Processor]
        Router --> InvProc[Invoice Processor]
        Router --> DispProc[Dispute Processor]
    end
    
    subgraph Data Stores
        Persist --> DB[(PostgreSQL)]
        PaymentProc --> DB
        SubProc --> DB
        InvProc --> DB
        DispProc --> DB
    end
    
    subgraph Domain Services
        PaymentProc --> PaySvc[Payment Service]
        SubProc --> SubSvc[Subscription Service]
        InvProc --> InvSvc[Invoice Service]
        DispProc --> DispSvc[Dispute Service]
    end
blueprints.notes: This diagram represents the v1 architecture with in-process event processing. Future versions may introduce an external message queue between ingestion and processing for better scalability.
